items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.q: Are monopolies always bad for consumers?
items.correct: Standard theory says monopolies restrict output and raise prices, but possible benefits include economies of scale, R&D investment and dynamic efficiency.
items.misconception: Monopolies are illegal and always harmful.
items.random_wrong: A monopoly is when one country dominates world trade.
items.why: Monopolies aren't automatically illegal — natural monopolies in utilities can be efficient. The analysis requires weighing static inefficiency against potential dynamic gains. Students treat the question as already settled.

items.q: Alfred Marshall published 'Principles of Economics' establishing supply-demand analysis in:
items.why: It's 1890. “1776” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A fall in the price of a substitute good causes demand to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decrease (curve shifts left). “Increase (shifts right)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 1890 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
